relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: The land promise is narrated through seven already-present peoples.
The ancient command moves into total destruction without covenant or mercy.
Marriage is restricted to protect religious allegiance within the conquest frame.
The fear is that intimate bonds will redirect worship.
Altars, pillars, sacred trees, and images are ordered destroyed.
Israel is named holy and treasured before YHWH.
Election does not arise from size or power.
It arises from love, ancestral promise, and liberation from slavery.
Covenant loyalty stretches through a thousand generations.
The same rhetoric promises direct repayment to enemies.
The people are called into practiced command.
Listening and doing are joined to remembered covenant loyalty.
Blessing spreads through people, soil, herd, and harvest.
Fertility is pictured as communal abundance, never a measure of individual worth.
Health is promised in rhetoric that must not become blame for illness.
The conquest voice again commands pitiless consumption of peoples.
Fear asks how a smaller people can prevail.
The answer returns to Exodus memory.
Liberation power is redirected toward conquest.
Even hidden survivors are imagined pursued.
Divine presence is invoked against terror.
The land will change hands gradually rather than all at once.
The nations are pictured falling into confusion.
Kings and names are threatened with erasure.
Sacred images and their wealth are rejected as snares.
The household is warned not to absorb what has been marked for destruction.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 7 contains both a treasure and a terror. The treasure is election grounded in love and liberation rather than strength. The terror is extermination, endogamy, and iconoclasm. Modern reception must carry the first and categorically refuse the second.
